response: The business-research problem is how to route capacitated vehicles to serve all customers at minimum monetary travel cost. Given customers, demands, travel costs, and vehicle capacities, the model chooses vehicle routes subject to vehicle capacity, customer service, and route continuity. Route consolidation and sequencing affect the stated monetary objective. The sustainability narrative is an objective mismatch: the model contains no emissions coefficients, environmental constraint, or empirical relationship between modeled cost and emissions. Lower monetary cost therefore does not necessarily mean lower carbon emissions. A faithful framing is cost-efficient vehicle routing, with any environmental implication explicitly labeled unsupported. To study sustainability, additional evidence would be needed linking route attributes to emissions; alternatively, emissions would need to be measured and incorporated as an objective, constraint, or reported outcome. None of that information is supplied, so no emissions-reduction or sustainability-advancement claim is warranted.
